title: WIPEOUT OF JIM’S ‘TINY TAX’ RELIEF
full_text:
Treasurer Jim Chalmers’ “tiny tax cut” is on the brink of being wiped out by a looming rate rise, with millions of mortgage holders facing another hit as the Coalition blames Labor spending for stoking inflation.
From today, every taxpayer gets at least $268 a year back via the government lowering the 16 per cent tax on incomes from $18,201 to $45,000 down to 15 per cent.
It works out at $5.15 a week, less than the price of a coffee. Amid hints of an interest rate rise by a further 0.25 as soon as August, that alone adds $114 a month – or $28 a week, according to Compare the Market – to the average mortgage, swallowing Mr Chalmers’ cost-of-living tax relief five times over. And that is without factoring in across-the-board food, fuel, power and council rates rises.
Meanwhile, a 0.25 hike would take the cash rate to its highest level since 2011. Still, Mr Chalmers heralded the $5.15 a week reprieve as “one of the five tax cuts” under Labor since 2022, claiming the average worker is better off by “more than $2800”.
“We’re delivering more cost of living help and we’re delivering real change in the tax system,” he told Sky News.
“From (today) we’ve got another tax cut, more wage increases, we’re extending paid parental leave, we’re paying super on payday and we are also extending fuel tax relief.” But an average 2022 salary of $96,267 would have had to rise 17 per cent since, or $16,365, to keep pace with inflation.
Opposition leader Angus Taylor said the tax cuts from today fail to meet inflation.
“It would not cover a cup of coffee a week. That is the difference. Labor lets inflation rip, then pretends a tiny tax cut is relief.
“Higher inflation means higher interest rates, and rates have already increased 15 times under Labor, leaving someone with a typical mortgage $29,000 a year worse off.” Based on those figures, the average mortgage holder pays $557 more a week while saving $54 a week in tax since 2022.
Mr Taylor said his Coalition would index tax brackets to inflation, giving “a typical worker around $400 back – we will give Australians their money back automatically every year.” Homeowners are being squeezed on both sides, with values falling faster as the Reserve Bank warns of rising interest rates again if inflation refuses to come under control.
Reserve Bank June meeting minutes reveal policymakers are prepared to lift the cash rate again, saying they will do whatever they “consider necessary” to return inflation to its 2-3 per cent target.
Sydney home values are down an average $31,000 since February’s first rate rise, with the pace of decline accelerating in June, new figures show.
It is $60b wiped off of Sydney property in total, based on 2021’s two million dwellings.
Sydney homes fell 0.5 per cent in June, higher than 0.2 per cent May decline, leaving prices 2.5 per cent below February levels. A median Sydney dwelling is worth $1.222m, down from $1.253m, PropTrack shows.
Many forecasters expect Sydney prices to keep falling, with SQM Research tipping a 9 per cent drop this year, with managing director Louis Christopher saying a six per cent forecast worsened by three per cent after the Budget.
ANZ forecasts an 8.4 per cent drop and Commonwealth Bank says 6 per cent, worsening if borrowing costs rise.
McGrath Estate Agents CEO John McGrath, based on agents at the coalface, says prices have plunged 10-15 per cent since March: “You would hope RBA says at some point enough is enough.
“The economy is in challenging, unstable times. People are struggling to survive. If (RBA) think we’ve got to go make things worse and put rates up again, that’s very odd.” REA Group economist Anne Flaherty said higher interest rates and uncertainty created by May Budget tax changes reduced buyer confidence and investor demand.
“There is also an expectation among home buyers that prices will continue to drop and that’s made a lot of them hesitant to buy,” she said.
The concern is greatest for first-home buyers this year via the Commonwealth 5 per cent deposit guarantee scheme.
Ms Flaherty warned further price falls could leave some in negative equity, owing more than their home value.
Housing Industry Association chief economist Tim Reardon said a construction pipeline “collapse” was “entirely caused by the government”, adding: “The number of contracts out is down, number of quotes being issued is down. We haven’t seen market confidence collapse this quickly since the pandemic.” KPMG chief economist Brendan Rynne says an August rate rise is “guaranteed”. EDITORIAL PAGE 21
